title: End-of-Chapter Quiz — Chapter 6: Materials Around Us
description: 10-question MCQ quiz. One question per screen. Correct tap = green + reason. Wrong tap = red shake + retry. Progress bar tracks all 10. Summary shows score, time, per-question results, and weakest topic to revise.

correct_answer_distribution.note: Correct answers are deliberately spread across all four positions to prevent pattern-guessing.

additionalProps.title: End-of-Chapter Quiz
additionalProps.subtitle: Answer each question — read the reason on every correct answer.

questions.question: Which statement best defines a "material"?

options.text: Any substance used to create or make an object
Only metals and plastics used in construction
Any living organism found in the environment
Any object that has a fixed and rigid shape

questions.reason: A material is any substance used to create objects — wood, glass, clay, metal, plastic, and more all qualify.
questions.hint: Think about what ALL objects — from a notebook to a bridge — are made FROM.

questions.question: Paper, wood, rubber, and jute are examples of which type of material?

options.text: Lustrous materials — they have a shiny surface
Transparent materials — light passes through them
Soluble materials — they dissolve in water easily
Non-lustrous materials — they do not have a shiny surface

questions.reason: Paper, wood, rubber, and jute are non-lustrous — their surfaces do not shine when light falls on them.
questions.hint: The opposite of lustrous. These materials have no natural shine or metallic glow.

questions.question: "All that glitters is not gold." What does this mean in the context of lustre?

options.text: Not all shiny materials are metals — some are coated with plastic or wax
All metals lose their lustre when exposed to air and moisture
Gold is the only truly lustrous metal in nature
Only freshly cut wood can appear shiny like a metal

questions.reason: Some non-metals are polished or coated with plastic or wax to appear shiny — lustre alone does not prove a material is a metal.
questions.hint: The saying warns us not to judge by appearance. Can non-metals look shiny too?

questions.question: Sheeta's brother watches the hide-and-seek game through a glass window. Why can he see clearly?

options.text: Glass is opaque — it reflects light so images appear on it
Glass is translucent — shapes are visible but blurry
Glass is transparent — objects can be seen clearly through it
Glass is lustrous — its shiny surface acts like a mirror

questions.reason: Glass is transparent — it allows light to pass through completely, so objects on the other side are seen clearly.
questions.hint: Transparent materials let ALL light through with no distortion — you can see details clearly.

questions.question: Which combination correctly classifies these three objects?

options.text: Eraser = transparent · Frosted glass = opaque · Clear glass = translucent
Eraser = opaque · Frosted glass = translucent · Clear glass = transparent
Eraser = translucent · Frosted glass = transparent · Clear glass = opaque
Eraser = lustrous · Frosted glass = soluble · Clear glass = hard

questions.reason: Eraser (opaque — no light through), frosted glass (translucent — blurry vision), clear glass (transparent — clear vision).
questions.hint: Opaque = no vision · Translucent = blurry vision · Transparent = clear vision. Match each material.

questions.question: Ghulan's mother prepares shikanji by mixing sugar, salt, and lemon juice in water. What happens to the sugar and salt?

options.text: They remain as visible particles at the bottom — they are insoluble
They float on the surface and separate from water over time
They turn the water into a solid after a few minutes
They dissolve completely in water — they are soluble

questions.reason: Sugar and salt are soluble — they dissolve completely in water, which is why shikanji tastes sweet and salty.
questions.hint: When something 'disappears' after being stirred in water, it has dissolved — the material is soluble.

questions.question: After stirring sand in water for a long time, you notice it settles at the bottom. Sand is:

options.text: Insoluble — it does not dissolve in water even after prolonged stirring
Soluble — it dissolves slowly and settles only when cold
Translucent — light passes through sand particles in water
Lustrous — sand particles reflect light from below the water

questions.reason: Sand is insoluble in water — it does not dissolve regardless of how long or hard you stir it.
questions.hint: Insoluble materials NEVER disappear in water. Settling at the bottom is the tell-tale sign.

questions.question: Three identical cups are half-filled — A with water, B with sand, C with pebbles. Cup C is heaviest. This shows different materials have different:

options.text: Volumes — pebbles occupy more space than water in the same cup
Lustre — pebbles reflect more light than sand or water
Mass — pebbles contain more matter per unit volume
Transparency — pebbles allow less light through than water

questions.reason: Mass is the quantity of matter. The same volume of different materials can have very different masses — pebbles have the greatest mass here.
questions.hint: The property measured in grams or kilograms that tells us HOW MUCH matter is in an object.

questions.question: Madam Vidya pours water into two identical glass tumblers. One is half-filled, the other nearly full. What differs between them?

options.text: Mass of the glass tumblers — one tumbler is physically heavier
Lustre of the water — more water makes it appear shinier
Solubility — more water increases the capacity to dissolve things
Volume of water — each tumbler holds a different amount of water

questions.reason: Volume is the space occupied by matter. The tumblers are identical — what differs is how much SPACE the water takes up inside each.
questions.hint: Volume = space occupied. The tumblers are the same; only the amount of water (space it fills) differs.

questions.question: Ayurveda uses 10 pairs of opposite guṇas to describe all matter. Which option lists a TRUE opposite pair from the Aṣhṭānga Hṛidaya?

options.text: Guru (heavy) and Snigdha (unctuous) — they are opposites
Mṛidu (soft) and Kaṭhina (hard) — they are opposites
Hima (cold) and Manda (slow) — they are opposites
Sūkṣhma (subtle) and Sthira (stable) — they are opposites

questions.reason: Mṛidu (soft) and Kaṭhina (hard) are one of the 10 true opposite guṇa pairs — directly mirroring soft/hard from Chapter 6.
questions.hint: Look for two words that are direct opposites in meaning. Which pair is clearly one extreme vs the other?

subtitle.default: Answer each question — read the reason on every correct answer.

questions.design_rule: Spread correctId values across a, b, c, d — never use the same position for more than 3 consecutive questions.

topic.description: Used for colour-coding and weakest-topic detection. Recommended values: materials | lustre | transparency | solubility | mass | volume | guṇa

correctId.description: Must match one of options[].id. Vary this across a/b/c/d throughout your question set.

reason.description: One-line explanation shown after correct tap. Keep ≤ 25 words.

hint.description: One-line guidance shown in summary for wrong answers. Keep ≤ 20 words.

agent_readme.correct_answer_rule: ALWAYS distribute correctId across all four positions (a, b, c, d). Never cluster correct answers — students will pattern-match. The default 10 questions use: a, d, a, c, b, d, a, c, d, b.
agent_readme.no_scrollbar_rule: The component uses flexbox with no fixed height — it grows to fit content. Never set a restrictive height in the parent container.
agent_readme.responsiveness: All font-sizes use clamp(). All paddings are relative. The component works from 320px to 1200px+ width.
agent_readme.retry_policy: Students can retry wrong options freely per question. Only a correct tap locks the answer and enables the Next button.

examples.name: Short 4-question drill — all different correct positions

questions.question: Which material allows you to see clearly through it?

options.text: Clear glass
Wooden plank
Brick wall
Metal sheet

questions.reason: Clear glass is transparent — objects on the other side are seen with full clarity.
questions.hint: Transparent = see clearly through. Which material does that?

questions.question: Which material is insoluble in water?

options.text: Sugar
Salt
Sand
Vinegar

questions.reason: Sand is insoluble — it never dissolves and remains separate even after prolonged stirring.
questions.hint: Which of these does NOT disappear when stirred into water?

questions.question: Mass is measured in which unit?

options.text: Litre (L)
Kilogram (kg)
Metre (m)
Celsius (°C)

questions.reason: Mass is measured in grams (g) and kilograms (kg) in the SI system.
questions.hint: Think about weighing objects on a scale — what unit is shown?

questions.question: 500 mL written on a water bottle refers to its:

options.text: Mass — how heavy the water is
Temperature — how warm the water is
Lustre — how shiny the bottle is
Volume — how much space the water occupies

questions.reason: mL (millilitre) is a unit of volume — it measures the space the water occupies inside the bottle.
questions.hint: mL and L are units of ___. What property do they measure?

instructions_for_student: Answer each of the ten questions. Tap an option — green means correct and you will see the reason. Red means try again! When you finish, see your score and which topic to revise.
